title: Backstory & Demo
subtitle: See the finished pipeline. Understand why single-LLM approaches fail for content production at scale.

concept.heading: What we're building
concept.body:
Housing.com publishes real estate content across Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, and their news blog — twice a day, every day. Each post needs to be platform-specific, trend-relevant, brand-safe, and SEO-optimised.

A single ChatGPT prompt produces something generic every time. We need something better.

callouts.title: The core problem
callouts.body: A single LLM call has no awareness of today's trends, no platform constraints, no brand voice, and no quality gate. It produces the same generic output regardless of context.

concept.heading: The destination
concept.body:
By the end of this course you will have built a 10-node LangGraph pipeline that:

- Researches 8 real estate news stories in parallel with 15 trending topics
- Filters them through a quality gate (no off-topic content)
- Generates Hinglish social copy, SEO articles, and platform-specific posts
- Runs 3-pass QA (safety → quality → engagement prediction)
- Publishes to 5 platforms or saves locally for review

callouts.title: The learning model
callouts.body: Every module adds ONE concept to a single pipeline.py file. By Module 12, that file IS the Housing.com agent.

endQuiz.stem: A single LLM call to 'write a tweet about real estate' fails primarily because:
options: It has no context about today's trends, platform constraints, or brand voice
endQuiz.explanation: Without context — current trends, platform rules, brand examples — the LLM defaults to generic safe output.

endQuiz.stem: In our pipeline, which agent filters out topics with no real estate angle (e.g. 'Coldplay concert')?
endQuiz.hint: It runs after research and before creative agents
endQuiz.explanation: The planner is the quality gate — it omits anything without a genuine housing angle.

endQuiz.stem: True or False: More LLM calls always means better output.
endQuiz.explanation: More calls add latency and cost. Only the RIGHT calls improve output — each agent must add specific value.

concept.heading: 10 nodes, one run
concept.body:
The pipeline runs in roughly this order:

**researcher** + **trend_researcher** run in parallel — Tavily web search for RE news, Serper/pytrends for viral topics.

**planner** waits for both, then filters and creates up to 8 content briefs.

**social_creative** + **news_creative** run in parallel — Hinglish social drafts (Opus 4.7) and SEO articles (Sonnet 4.6).

**platform_agents** format each draft for Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, LinkedIn, Housing News — all simultaneously.

**qa_agent** runs 3 passes: safety gate → quality scoring → engagement prediction. Failing posts get revised.

**publisher** saves to disk (dry run) or posts live.

**notifier** posts a Slack summary.

callouts.title: Why parallel?
callouts.body: researcher and trend_researcher both take ~15 seconds. Running them sequentially = 30s. Parallel = 15s. At 2 runs/day, that's 15 minutes saved per month just on research.

callouts.title: Cost matters
callouts.body: Using Opus 4.7 for every call costs ~$12/run. Model routing brings it to ~$0.85. 2 runs/day for 30 days: $720 vs $51.

endQuiz.stem: Which part of the pipeline uses the most expensive model (Opus 4.7)?
endQuiz.explanation: social_creative uses Opus 4.7 because creative quality directly impacts engagement. Other nodes use cheaper models appropriate to their task.

endQuiz.stem: What is the QA agent's 'revision' decision?
options: Send the post back to the platform agent with specific fix instructions
Ask a human to rewrite it
Publish it anyway with a warning
endQuiz.explanation: A fixable post (e.g. 312 chars instead of 280) gets sent back to the platform agent with locked elements preserved and specific fix instructions.
